title: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
content:
Type 2 Diabetes Management Protocol:

Diagnosis Criteria (ADA):
- Fasting Plasma Glucose ≥126 mg/dL
- HbA1c ≥6.5%
- 2-hour PG ≥200 mg/dL during OGTT
- Random PG ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms

Treatment Algorithm:
1. Step 1: Lifestyle modifications + Metformin (first-line)
   - Start 500mg once or twice daily with meals
   - Maximum 2,550mg/day in divided doses
   - Monitor renal function (eGFR)
   - Hold before and 48h after iodinated contrast
2. Step 2: Add second agent based on patient profile:
   - Cardiovascular disease → SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) or GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide)
   - Obesity → GLP-1 RA
   - Cost concern → Sulfonylurea or TZD
3. Step 3: Triple therapy or insulin initiation

Metformin Key Points:
- Mechanism: Decreases hepatic glucose production, improves insulin sensitivity
- Contraindications: eGFR <30 mL/min, metabolic acidosis, diabetic ketoacidosis
- Common side effects: Nausea, diarrhea, flatulence (usually transient)
- Drug interaction: Caution with carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (lactic acidosis risk)

Monitoring:
- HbA1c every 3 months (target <7% for most adults)
- Annual: eye exam, foot exam, renal function, lipid panel
- Self-monitoring of blood glucose as needed

title: ACC/AHA Hypertension Guidelines (2017)
content:
Hypertension Management Protocol:

Diagnosis Threshold:
- Stage 1: ≥130/80 mmHg
- Stage 2: ≥140/90 mmHg

First-line Medications:
1. ACE Inhibitors (Enalapril, Lisinopril) — preferred for diabetes/CKD patients
2. ARBs (Losartan, Valsartan) — alternative to ACEi if cough develops
3. Calcium Channel Blockers (Amlodipine, Nifedipine) — preferred for African American patients
4. Thiazide Diuretics (Hydrochlorothiazide, Chlorthalidone)

Lifestyle Modifications:
- DASH diet, sodium restriction (<2.3g/day)
- Regular exercise (150 min/week moderate intensity)
- Weight management (target BMI <25)
- Limit alcohol (<2 drinks/day men, <1 women)
- Smoking cessation

Combination Therapy:
- If BP >20/10 above target, start with 2-drug combination
- ACEi + CCB or ACEi + Thiazide are preferred combinations
- NEVER combine ACEi + ARB (hyperkalemia risk)

Follow-up: Recheck BP in 1 month after initiation/adjustment
Target: <130/80 for most adults, <140/90 for >65 years

title: Clinical Pharmacology Drug Interactions Reference
content:
Common Drug Interactions in Primary Care:

1. Warfarin + NSAIDs — Increased bleeding risk. Use acetaminophen instead. Monitor INR closely if unavoidable.
2. ACEi/ARBs + Potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone) — Risk of hyperkalemia. Monitor K+ levels within 1 week.
3. SSRIs + NSAIDs — Increased GI bleeding risk (both inhibit platelet function). Consider PPI co-prescription.
4. Statins + Macrolides (clarithromycin, erythromycin) — Increased myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk via CYP3A4 inhibition.
5. Metformin + Iodinated contrast dye — Risk of lactic acidosis. Hold metformin 48h before and after.
6. Ciprofloxacin + Theophylline — Increased theophylline toxicity (inhibits CYP1A2). Reduce theophylline dose 30-50%.
7. Beta-blockers + Verapamil/Diltiazem — Risk of severe bradycardia and heart block. Avoid combination.
8. Digoxin + Amiodarone — Amiodarone increases digoxin levels 70-100%. Reduce digoxin dose by half.
9. Clopidogrel + Omeprazole — Omeprazole reduces clopidogrel activation. Use pantoprazole instead.
10. Lithium + NSAIDs/ACEi/Thiazides — All increase lithium levels. Monitor lithium levels closely.

title: GINA 2024 Report
content:
Asthma Management (GINA 2024):

Stepwise Approach:
- Step 1-2: As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol (PREFERRED) or as-needed SABA with low-dose ICS
- Step 3: Low-dose ICS-LABA maintenance + as-needed ICS-formoterol
- Step 4: Medium-dose ICS-LABA maintenance
- Step 5: High-dose ICS-LABA ± add-on therapy (tiotropium, biologics like omalizumab)

Key Changes in GINA 2024:
- SABA-only treatment is NO LONGER RECOMMENDED as sole therapy
- All adults/adolescents with asthma should receive ICS-containing therapy
- ICS-formoterol is preferred reliever at all steps

Monitoring:
- Peak flow monitoring for moderate-severe patients
- Written asthma action plan for ALL patients
- Review inhaler technique at every visit
- Assess control: daytime symptoms, night waking, reliever use, activity limitation

Emergency Management:
- Severe exacerbation: Nebulized SABA + ipratropium + systemic corticosteroids + oxygen
- If no improvement in 1 hour: consider IV magnesium sulfate
- ICU if: drowsy, confused, silent chest, PEF <25% predicted

title: WHO Model List of Essential Medicines (2023)
content:
Common Analgesic Dosing Guidelines:

Paracetamol (Acetaminophen):
- Adult: 500-1000mg every 4-6 hours, max 4g/day
- Pediatric: 10-15 mg/kg every 4-6 hours
- Hepatotoxicity risk above 4g/day or with chronic alcohol use
- Safe in pregnancy (all trimesters)

Ibuprofen:
- Adult: 200-400mg every 4-6 hours, max 1.2g/day (OTC) or 2.4g/day (prescription)
- Take with food to reduce GI side effects
- Contraindications: active GI ulcer, severe renal impairment, third trimester pregnancy
- Caution in elderly (renal, cardiovascular risk)

NSAID Safety:
- All NSAIDs carry cardiovascular risk (except low-dose aspirin)
- Naproxen may have lowest CV risk among NSAIDs
- GI protection (PPI) recommended if: age >65, history of ulcer, concurrent anticoagulant/steroid
- Avoid in CKD stage 4-5 (eGFR <30)

title: ICMR Guidelines for Management of T2DM
content:
ICMR Diabetes Management for Indian Population:

Dietary Recommendations:
- Reduce refined carbohydrates (white rice, maida)
- Increase fiber intake (whole grains, vegetables, dals)
- Limit fruit juice (prefer whole fruits, 2 servings/day)
- Cooking oil: <20g/day, prefer mustard/groundnut/rice bran oil
- Salt: <5g/day

Physical Activity:
- 150 minutes/week moderate activity (brisk walking)
- Resistance training 2-3 times/week
- Reduce prolonged sitting (break every 30 minutes)

Screening:
- Annual screening for all adults >30 with risk factors
- HbA1c or fasting glucose preferred
- Screen for complications annually: retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy, foot exam

Indian-Specific Considerations:
- Indians develop diabetes at lower BMI (threshold: BMI >23 for overweight)
- Higher prevalence of insulin resistance
- Genetic predisposition (family history very significant)
- Urban lifestyle change is a major risk factor
